feat: LISR-8 complete — documentation, governance, and maintenance playbooks

LISR-8.1: Architecture ADR (LisrAdr) with threat model (6 threats),
safety invariants (6), fail-closed rationale, and design decision.

LISR-8.2: Operator playbook (OperatorPlaybook) with mode guidance,
canary rollout procedure, and incident response steps.

LISR-8.3: Developer guide (DeveloperGuide) with add-rule checklist,
anti-patterns, and testing expectations.

LISR-8.4: Governance checklist (run_governance_checklist) with 6
compile-time checks verifying registry, ADR, threats, invariants,
and testing documentation.

All 8 LISR epics complete. 326 tests passing, zero clippy warnings.

 {"id":"bd-k5q5.8.6","title":"Pattern 5: export shape normalization for CJS/ESM translation issues","description":"## Problem

Some extensions fail with "not a function" when the runtime tries to call
the default export.  This happens because of CJS/ESM translation mismatches:

- Extension uses \`module.exports = function(pi) { ... }\` (CJS pattern)
- Our maybe_cjs_to_esm() converts this but sometimes produces
  \`export default { default: function(pi) { ... } }\` (double-wrapped)
- Or the extension uses \`exports.activate = function(pi) { ... }\` instead
  of a default export

### Real examples
- npm/aliou-pi-guardrails: failed because configLoader.getConfig() wasn't
  a function (now fixed by ConfigLoader stub, but the general pattern persists)
- npm/aliou-pi-toolchain: same ConfigLoader issue
- Some community extensions use \`export function activate(pi)\` instead of
  \`export default function(pi)\`

## Solution

In the \`__pi_load_extension\` JS function (extensions_js.rs), add fallback
logic when calling the default export fails:

```javascript
async function __pi_load_extension(entryPath, metadata) {
    const mod = await import(entryPath);

    // Try calling default export
    let activator = mod.default;

    // Pattern 5a: double-wrapped default
    if (activator && typeof activator !== 'function' && typeof activator.default === 'function') {
        activator = activator.default;
        // emit repair event
    }

    // Pattern 5b: named activate/init export
    if (typeof activator !== 'function') {
        if (typeof mod.activate === 'function') {
            activator = mod.activate;
            // emit repair event
        } else if (typeof mod.init === 'function') {
            activator = mod.init;
            // emit repair event
        } else if (typeof mod.setup === 'function') {
            activator = mod.setup;
            // emit repair event
        }
    }

    if (typeof activator !== 'function') {
        throw new Error(
            'Extension default export is not a function. ' +
            'Expected: export default function(pi) { ... } or ' +
            'export function activate(pi) { ... }'
        );
    }

    await activator(__pi_create_api(metadata));
}
```

## Security Analysis

- SAFE: We're only changing WHICH function gets called, not granting any
  new capabilities.  The function still receives the same sandboxed API object.
- The fallback only tries a fixed set of known lifecycle method names
  (default, activate, init, setup).  No arbitrary property access.
- If none of the known patterns match, the extension still fails with a
  clear error message.

## Also: improve maybe_cjs_to_esm()

The root cause of some double-wrapping is in maybe_cjs_to_esm() which
handles \`module.exports = ...\`.  We should audit this function to ensure
it doesn't produce double-wrapped defaults.  Specifically:

- \`module.exports = fn\` → \`export default fn\` (correct)
- \`module.exports = { default: fn }\` → should unwrap to \`export default fn\`
- \`exports.default = fn\` → \`export default fn\` (correct)

## Testing

1. Unit test: double-wrapped default is unwrapped
2. Unit test: named activate export is used as fallback
3. Unit test: named init export is used as fallback
4. Unit test: completely non-callable export still throws clear error
5. Unit test: repair event emitted with ExportShape pattern
6. Unit test: maybe_cjs_to_esm doesn't double-wrap module.exports = { default: fn }
7. Regression: all 223 extensions still pass

## Estimated Effort: 2-3 hours

## Files to Modify
- src/extensions_js.rs (__pi_load_extension JS function, maybe_cjs_to_esm)

- Requires bd-k5q5.8.1 (logging infrastructure) for repair event emission
- Independent of Patterns 1-4","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:18.988843552Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:36:52.711010052Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["auto-repair","cjs-esm","extensions","module-loading"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.6","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:18.988843552Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.6","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:45.408447637Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":868,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.6","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Implementation Details: Export Shape Normalization

### The Problem
Some extensions export their lifecycle functions in non-standard ways due to
CJS/ESM translation issues. The most common scenarios:
- Default export is an object wrapping the init function: `{ default: { init } }`
- Default export is the init function directly but module.exports isn't set
- Named exports exist but default export is undefined
- Double-wrapping from bundler output: `{ default: { default: init } }`

### Affected Extensions
- aliou-pi-guardrails: `init` was not callable due to export shape
- aliou-pi-toolchain: similar CJS/ESM mismatch
- Various npm-registry extensions with bundler output

### Current Manual Fix
In the conformance test runner and extension loader, we rely on the module having
a callable default export with an `init` method. Extensions that don't match this
shape simply fail. The manual fix was correcting VALIDATED_MANIFEST.json to not
expect registrations from extensions that fail this way.

### Auto-Repair Implementation

Location: Extension loader in `src/extensions_js.rs`, specifically where we
call the extension's init/activate function.

After loading the module, before calling init:
```javascript
function normalizeExtensionExport(mod) {
    // Already a function with init → use as-is
    if (typeof mod === 'function') return mod;
    if (typeof mod?.init === 'function') return mod;

    // Unwrap { default: X } from CJS interop
    if (mod?.default) {
        if (typeof mod.default === 'function') return mod.default;
        if (typeof mod.default?.init === 'function') return mod.default;
        // Double-wrapped: { default: { default: X } }
        if (mod.default?.default) {
            if (typeof mod.default.default === 'function') return mod.default.default;
            if (typeof mod.default.default?.init === 'function') return mod.default.default;
        }
    }

    // Try named exports as fallback
    if (typeof mod?.activate === 'function') return { init: mod.activate };
    if (typeof mod?.setup === 'function') return { init: mod.setup };

    return mod; // Give up, return original (will fail naturally)
}
```

### Where This Runs
In `run_extension_init()` or equivalent in extensions_js.rs, after the module
is loaded via QuickJS but before we call `.init(api)`:
```rust
// After eval_module():
let raw_export = get_default_export(module);
// Apply normalization:
let normalized = call_normalize_extension_export(raw_export);
// Now call init:
call_init(normalized, api);
```

### Heuristic Priority (highest to lowest)
1. Direct callable with init method (standard) - no repair needed
2. Unwrap one level of { default: X } - very common CJS interop pattern
3. Unwrap two levels of { default: { default: X } } - bundler artifacts
4. Try 'activate' named export (VS Code extension convention)
5. Try 'setup' named export (alternative convention)
6. Give up and let original error propagate

### Security Notes
- Normalization only restructures the export object, no new code execution
- The init function that gets called is still the extension's own code
- We're just finding the right reference to call, not injecting behavior
- Every normalization step is logged as a repair event

### Testing
- Unit: standard export shape → no normalization applied
- Unit: { default: initFn } → unwraps correctly
- Unit: { default: { default: initFn } } → double-unwrap works
- Unit: { activate: fn } → wrapped as { init: fn }
- Unit: no valid shape → returns original (natural failure)
- Unit: repair_config.export_shape = false → no normalization
- Integration: load aliou-pi-guardrails → succeeds via normalization","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:36:52Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","title":"Integration tests: verify auto-repair with real extensions","description":"## What

After all 5 patterns are implemented, create an integration test suite that
verifies auto-repair works end-to-end with real extension artifacts.  The test
should:

1. Remove the manual stubs we created (shared/index.ts, dist/extension.js,
   form/*.html, constants.ts, utils.ts, processes-component.ts)
2. Load the previously-broken extensions
3. Verify they load successfully via auto-repair
4. Verify the correct repair events are emitted
5. Verify the extensions register the expected commands/tools/hooks

## Test Structure

\`tests/extension_auto_repair.rs\`:

```rust
#[test]
fn auto_repair_dist_to_src_fallback() {
    // Remove dist/extension.js stub for community/qualisero-pi-agent-scip
    // Load the extension
    // Verify it loads and registers expected items
    // Verify RepairEvent(DistToSrc) was emitted
}

#[test]
fn auto_repair_missing_asset() {
    // Remove form/ stubs for nicobailon-interview-tool
    // Load the extension
    // Verify it loads (server.ts gets empty HTML/CSS/JS)
    // Verify RepairEvent(MissingAsset) was emitted
}

#[test]
fn auto_repair_monorepo_escape() {
    // Remove shared/index.ts stub
    // Load qualisero-background-notify
    // Verify it loads with auto-generated stub
    // Verify RepairEvent(MonorepoEscape) was emitted
}

#[test]
fn auto_repair_proxy_stub() {
    // Remove a hand-written virtual module
    // Load extension that depends on it
    // Verify it loads via proxy stub
    // Verify RepairEvent(MissingNpmDep) was emitted
}

#[test]
fn auto_repair_disabled() {
    // Set auto_repair_enabled = false
    // Try loading a broken extension
    // Verify it fails with the original error
    // Verify no repair events emitted
}

#[test]
fn auto_repair_never_stubs_system_modules() {
    // Verify that node:fs, child_process, etc. are never proxy-stubbed
    // Even when auto_repair is enabled
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] At least 6 integration tests covering all 5 patterns + disabled mode
- [ ] Tests use real extension artifacts (not synthetic)
- [ ] Tests verify both success (extension loads) and observability (events emitted)
- [ ] Tests verify the kill-switch works (auto_repair_enabled = false)
- [ ] All tests pass in CI

## Why This Matters

The integration tests are our proof that auto-repair works correctly and safely.
They're also the regression safety net — if a future change breaks auto-repair,
these tests catch it.

## Estimated Effort: 3-4 hours

## Files to Create
- tests/extension_auto_repair.rs

## Dependencies
- Requires ALL 5 patterns to be implemented
- Requires bd-k5q5.8.1 (logging), bd-k5q5.8.2 (P1), bd-k5q5.8.3 (P2),
  bd-k5q5.8.4 (P3), bd-k5q5.8.5 (P4), bd-k5q5.8.6 (P5)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:14.532673214Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:37:08.298334621Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["auto-repair","extensions","integration","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:14.532673214Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:18.302062857Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:21.559483168Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:24.855352435Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:27.478677090Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:29.791234320Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":869,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Implementation Details: Integration Tests

### Test Strategy
Integration tests verify the auto-repair system works end-to-end with REAL
extension source code from the conformance corpus. They are the gatekeeper
before we graduate manual stubs (bd-k5q5.8.8).

### Test Structure
File: `tests/ext_auto_repair.rs` (new file, feature-gated with `ext-conformance`)

### Test Scenarios

**1. dist→src fallback (Pattern 1)**
- Remove the manual `dist/extension.js` stub from qualisero-pi-agent-scip
- Load the extension with auto-repair enabled
- Assert: extension loads successfully
- Assert: RepairEvent with pattern=DistToSrc is emitted
- Assert: the resolved path points to `src/extension.ts`

**2. Missing asset fallback (Pattern 2)**
- Remove the manual `form/` directory from nicobailon-interview-tool
- Load the extension with auto-repair enabled
- Assert: extension loads (readFileSync returns empty string)
- Assert: RepairEvent with pattern=MissingAsset is emitted for each file

**3. Monorepo escape (Pattern 3)**
- Remove the manual `shared/index.ts` stub
- Load qualisero-background-notify with auto-repair enabled
- Assert: extension loads successfully
- Assert: RepairEvent with pattern=MonorepoEscape is emitted
- Assert: the generated stub exports match what the extension imports

**4. npm proxy (Pattern 4)**
- Temporarily remove `@sourcegraph/scip-python` from virtual modules
- Load scip extension with auto-repair enabled
- Assert: extension loads, proxy stub provides no-op responses
- Assert: RepairEvent with pattern=NpmProxy is emitted

**5. Export shape normalization (Pattern 5)**
- Construct a test extension with CJS-style double-wrapped export
- Load with auto-repair enabled
- Assert: init() is found and called correctly
- Assert: RepairEvent with pattern=ExportShape is emitted

**6. All repairs disabled**
- Set all RepairConfig flags to false
- Load an extension that needs repair
- Assert: it FAILS (repairs don't fire when disabled)

**7. Repair event audit**
- Load multiple extensions that need different repairs
- Assert: repair_events() returns the correct count and patterns
- Assert: no false-positive repairs (extensions that load cleanly have no events)

**8. New extension validation**
- Download 2-3 extensions NOT in current corpus (from the 24 identified repos)
- Load them with auto-repair enabled
- Document which repairs fire (regression test for future)

### Test Infrastructure
- Uses the same `load_and_validate_extension()` from conformance_report.rs
- Adds `repair_events()` method to the test harness
- Feature-gated: only runs with `--features ext-conformance`
- Each test restores the original state (doesn't permanently remove stubs)

### Success Criteria
- All 8 test scenarios pass
- Repair events are accurate (no false positives)
- At least 1 NEW extension (not in corpus) loads via auto-repair","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:37:08Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-k5q5.8.8","title":"Graduate manual stubs: remove hand-written fixes replaced by auto-repair","description":"## What

After auto-repair is implemented and integration-tested, remove the manual
stub files we created to get to 223/223.  The auto-repair pipeline should
handle these cases dynamically, so the stubs become dead code.

## Manual stubs to remove

1. \`tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/shared/index.ts\`
   - Created for: qualisero-background-notify, qualisero-safe-git
   - Replaced by: Pattern 3 (monorepo escape stub generation)

2. \`tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/community/qualisero-pi-agent-scip/dist/extension.js\`
   - Created for: community/qualisero-pi-agent-scip
   - Replaced by: Pattern 1 (dist→src fallback)

3. \`tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/community/nicobailon-interview-tool/form/\`
   - Created for: nicobailon-interview-tool
   - Replaced by: Pattern 2 (missing asset fallback)

4. \`tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/npm/aliou-pi-processes/constants.ts\`
   \`tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/npm/aliou-pi-processes/utils.ts\`
   \`tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/npm/aliou-pi-processes/components/processes-component.ts\`
   - Created for: aliou-pi-processes
   - Replaced by: Pattern 3 (monorepo escape) for most, Pattern 2 for some

## Process

1. For each stub, verify that the corresponding auto-repair pattern handles it
2. Remove the stub file
3. Run conformance_full_report to verify the extension still passes
4. Verify the repair event shows the correct pattern
5. Commit the removal

## Why NOT to remove all stubs at once

Some stubs may turn out to be better than auto-repair for specific cases.
For example, the shared/index.ts has carefully typed interfaces that the
auto-generated stub won't have.  If an extension's runtime behavior depends
on the stub having correct types/values, keep the manual stub and document why.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Each removable stub is identified and tagged
- [ ] Each removal is verified with a conformance run
- [ ] Stubs that are better kept are documented with reasons
- [ ] No regression in conformance results

## Estimated Effort: 1-2 hours

## Dependencies
- Requires bd-k5q5.8.7 (integration tests passing)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:50.890583943Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:37:28.870065180Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["auto-repair","cleanup","extensions"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.8","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:50.890583943Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.8","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5.8.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:31:52.831794974Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":870,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.8.8","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Implementation Details: Graduate Manual Stubs

### What This Means
Once auto-repair is implemented and integration-tested, we can remove the
hand-written stubs that were created to achieve 100% conformance. The auto-repair
system should generate equivalent behavior dynamically.

### Manual Artifacts to Remove

**1. Filesystem Stubs (Pattern 1 & 2 replacements)**
- `tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/community/qualisero-pi-agent-scip/dist/extension.js`
  → Auto-repair Pattern 1 handles this (dist→src fallback)
- `tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/community/nicobailon-interview-tool/form/` (7 files)
  → Auto-repair Pattern 2 handles this (missing asset fallback)

**2. Monorepo Stubs (Pattern 3 replacement)**
- `tests/ext_conformance/artifacts/shared/index.ts` (17 exports)
  → Auto-repair Pattern 3 generates this dynamically from import analysis

**3. Virtual Module Stubs (Pattern 4 replacement - SELECTIVE)**
Some virtual modules should be KEPT because they provide nuanced behavior:
- KEEP: node:fs, node:path, node:os (system module shims with real behavior)
- KEEP: `@marckrenn/pi-sub-shared` (has specific MODEL_MULTIPLIERS data)
- REMOVE: Generic stubs that just export empty objects/functions
- Candidates for removal:
  - `@sourcegraph/scip-python` (proxy would work fine)
  - Simple no-op stubs that match the proxy pattern exactly

### Migration Process
For each stub removal:
1. Remove the stub (file or virtual module entry)
2. Run conformance tests with auto-repair enabled
3. Verify the extension still passes
4. Verify the RepairEvent matches the expected pattern
5. If test fails: investigate why and either improve auto-repair OR keep the stub

### Rollback Plan
- Keep all removed stubs in a git tag for easy rollback
- If auto-repair regresses, re-add the affected stubs
- Feature flags allow disabling individual patterns without code changes

### NOT in Scope
- Removing VALIDATED_MANIFEST.json fixes (those correct genuine expectation mismatches)
- Removing crypto_shim.rs (that provides real node:crypto functionality)
- Removing node:* module shims (those provide real behavior, not just stubs)

### Success Criteria
- At least 5 manual stubs removed and replaced by auto-repair
- Conformance still at 223/223 (or higher if new extensions added)
- No manual intervention needed for the graduated extensions
- Repair events logged for each auto-repaired extension","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:37:28Z"}]}
-{"id":"bd-k5q5.9","title":"[LISR] Dynamic Secure Extension Repair Program (Intent-Legible Self-Healing)","description":"This program introduces a secure, deterministic, and auditable mechanism to dynamically repair broken extensions when intent is legible and risk can be bounded. Current extension breakage often comes from schema drift, API renames, and minor structural issues; we want a first-class self-healing path that reduces operator toil without compromising safety.

This epic exists to coordinate all required workstreams: policy and threat boundaries, legibility scoring, deterministic rewrites, constrained model assistance, behavior proofs, controlled rollout, and operational observability.","design":"Architecture is phase-gated and fail-closed:
1) Security contract and policy modes define what is allowed.
2) Intent legibility pipeline decides whether auto-repair is even eligible.
3) Deterministic rule engine performs known-safe rewrites first.
4) Optional model-assisted proposal stage is constrained by a primitive whitelist.
5) Proof pipeline verifies behavioral and capability invariants.
6) Overlay canary rollout validates production safety before promotion.
7) Full audit and telemetry enable forensic confidence and operator trust.","acceptance_criteria":"- End-to-end repair path exists from broken extension input to safe promoted output.
- Repair never increases privilege without explicit human approval.
- Validation/proof pipeline blocks unsafe or ambiguous repairs.
- Canary/rollback controls are automated and deterministic.
- Audit trail is complete enough for security review and incident forensics.","notes":"Strategic justification:
- Improves reliability and user trust in extension ecosystem.
- Reduces mean-time-to-recovery for breakages caused by upstream/API drift.
- Preserves project principle: safety and determinism over convenience.

Non-goals:
- No unconstrained AI patching.
- No silent privilege expansion.
- No executing untrusted broken code to infer intent.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:13.622771644Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:35:25.741429171Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","lisr","program","repair","security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:13.622771644Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":833,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Program context: this issue captures the complete self-healing strategy for extension repair when intent is legible and risk is bounded. Every child issue includes embedded rationale so future contributors do not need external planning documents.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:33:08Z"},{"id":845,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Execution map and dependency rationale:\n\nPhase A (Policy foundation): LISR-1 defines mode semantics, static-only boundaries, and privilege monotonicity. Every downstream epic depends on this because no repair behavior is acceptable without enforceable safety policy.\n\nPhase B (Eligibility and deterministic repair): LISR-2 determines whether intent is legible enough to repair. LISR-3 performs deterministic rewrites for known breakage classes. These two epics establish the safe default path and reduce reliance on model assistance.\n\nPhase C (Constrained AI path): LISR-4 is explicitly downstream of LISR-2 and LISR-3. We only allow model proposals after deterministic options and confidence gating are available, and only through strict primitive schemas.\n\nPhase D (Proof before activation): LISR-5 blocks production rollout. Structural checks, capability monotonicity, hostcall parity, and conformance replay/golden checksums are the hard activation gate.\n\nPhase E (Runtime rollout safety): LISR-6 adds overlay artifacts, canary routing, and deterministic rollback. This assumes proof pipeline outputs are trustworthy and machine-readable.\n\nPhase F (Operational trust): LISR-7 captures append-only ledger, telemetry, operator inspection, and forensic bundles to support incident response and governance.\n\nPhase G (Institutional memory): LISR-8 codifies ADRs, playbooks, developer guidance, and release governance so system intent survives team turnover.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:34:43Z"},{"id":856,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Detailed phase rationale (human-readable version):

Phase A - Policy Foundation:
LISR-1 is upstream of all work because no repair automation is acceptable without mode semantics, static-only boundaries, and privilege monotonicity controls.

Phase B - Legibility + Deterministic Repair:
LISR-2 determines eligibility (is intent legible enough?) and LISR-3 provides deterministic, auditable repair behavior for common breakage classes.

Phase C - Constrained AI Assist:
LISR-4 is intentionally downstream of LISR-2 and LISR-3 so model usage remains bounded, policy-governed, and secondary to deterministic fixes.

Phase D - Proof Gate:
LISR-5 is the hard activation barrier. Candidates must pass structural checks, security invariants, semantic parity checks, and conformance replay.

Phase E - Runtime Safety:
LISR-6 delivers canary and rollback controls to handle real-world runtime regressions even after offline proof passes.

Phase F - Trust and Operations:
LISR-7 ensures every decision is observable, explainable, and reconstructable for incidents.

Phase G - Long-Term Maintainability:
LISR-8 captures rationale, operator procedures, and governance so future teams can evolve the system safely.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:35:25Z"}]}

2) Intent legibility pipeline decides whether auto-repair is even eligible.
3) Deterministic rule engine performs known-safe rewrites first.
4) Optional model-assisted proposal stage is constrained by a primitive whitelist.
5) Proof pipeline verifies behavioral and capability invariants.
6) Overlay canary rollout validates production safety before promotion.
7) Full audit and telemetry enable forensic confidence and operator trust.","acceptance_criteria":"- End-to-end repair path exists from broken extension input to safe promoted output.
- Repair never increases privilege without explicit human approval.
- Validation/proof pipeline blocks unsafe or ambiguous repairs.
- Canary/rollback controls are automated and deterministic.
- Audit trail is complete enough for security review and incident forensics.","notes":"Strategic justification:
- Improves reliability and user trust in extension ecosystem.
- Reduces mean-time-to-recovery for breakages caused by upstream/API drift.
- Preserves project principle: safety and determinism over convenience.

Non-goals:
- No unconstrained AI patching.
- No silent privilege expansion.
- No executing untrusted broken code to infer intent.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:13.622771644Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-09T00:24:57.846608742Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T00:24:57.846509187Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["extensions","lisr","program","repair","security"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","depends_on_id":"bd-k5q5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:30:13.622771644Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":833,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Program context: this issue captures the complete self-healing strategy for extension repair when intent is legible and risk is bounded. Every child issue includes embedded rationale so future contributors do not need external planning documents.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:33:08Z"},{"id":845,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Execution map and dependency rationale:\n\nPhase A (Policy foundation): LISR-1 defines mode semantics, static-only boundaries, and privilege monotonicity. Every downstream epic depends on this because no repair behavior is acceptable without enforceable safety policy.\n\nPhase B (Eligibility and deterministic repair): LISR-2 determines whether intent is legible enough to repair. LISR-3 performs deterministic rewrites for known breakage classes. These two epics establish the safe default path and reduce reliance on model assistance.\n\nPhase C (Constrained AI path): LISR-4 is explicitly downstream of LISR-2 and LISR-3. We only allow model proposals after deterministic options and confidence gating are available, and only through strict primitive schemas.\n\nPhase D (Proof before activation): LISR-5 blocks production rollout. Structural checks, capability monotonicity, hostcall parity, and conformance replay/golden checksums are the hard activation gate.\n\nPhase E (Runtime rollout safety): LISR-6 adds overlay artifacts, canary routing, and deterministic rollback. This assumes proof pipeline outputs are trustworthy and machine-readable.\n\nPhase F (Operational trust): LISR-7 captures append-only ledger, telemetry, operator inspection, and forensic bundles to support incident response and governance.\n\nPhase G (Institutional memory): LISR-8 codifies ADRs, playbooks, developer guidance, and release governance so system intent survives team turnover.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:34:43Z"},{"id":856,"issue_id":"bd-k5q5.9","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Detailed phase rationale (human-readable version):

Phase A - Policy Foundation:
LISR-1 is upstream of all work because no repair automation is acceptable without mode semantics, static-only boundaries, and privilege monotonicity controls.

Phase B - Legibility + Deterministic Repair:
LISR-2 determines eligibility (is intent legible enough?) and LISR-3 provides deterministic, auditable repair behavior for common breakage classes.

Phase C - Constrained AI Assist:
LISR-4 is intentionally downstream of LISR-2 and LISR-3 so model usage remains bounded, policy-governed, and secondary to deterministic fixes.

Phase D - Proof Gate:
LISR-5 is the hard activation barrier. Candidates must pass structural checks, security invariants, semantic parity checks, and conformance replay.

Phase E - Runtime Safety:
LISR-6 delivers canary and rollback controls to handle real-world runtime regressions even after offline proof passes.

Phase F - Trust and Operations:
LISR-7 ensures every decision is observable, explainable, and reconstructable for incidents.

Phase G - Long-Term Maintainability:
LISR-8 captures rationale, operator procedures, and governance so future teams can evolve the system safely.","created_at":"2026-02-08T21:35:25Z"}]}

+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Architecture ADR and threat-model rationale (bd-k5q5.9.8.1)
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Architecture Decision Record for the LISR system.
+///
+/// Records why LISR exists, what threats it addresses, and why fail-closed
+/// constraints were chosen. This is embedded in code to prevent drift from
+/// the original safety intent.
+pub struct LisrAdr;
+
+impl LisrAdr {
+    /// ADR identifier.
+    pub const ID: &'static str = "ADR-LISR-001";
+
+    /// Title of the architecture decision.
+    pub const TITLE: &'static str =
+        "Dynamic Secure Extension Repair with Intent-Legible Self-Healing";
+
+    /// Why LISR exists.
+    pub const CONTEXT: &'static str = "\
+Extensions frequently break during updates when build artifacts (dist/) \
+diverge from source (src/). Manual repair is slow, error-prone, and blocks \
+the agent workflow. LISR provides automated repair within strict safety \
+boundaries to restore extension functionality without human intervention.";
+
+    /// The core architectural decision.
+    pub const DECISION: &'static str = "\
+Adopt a layered repair pipeline with fail-closed defaults: \
+(1) security policy framework bounds all repairs, \
+(2) intent legibility analysis gates repair eligibility, \
+(3) deterministic rules execute safe repairs, \
+(4) model-assisted repairs are constrained to whitelisted primitives, \
+(5) all repairs require structural + capability + semantic proof, \
+(6) overlay deployment uses canary routing with health rollback, \
+(7) every action is recorded in an append-only audit ledger, \
+(8) governance checks are codified in the release process.";
+
+    /// Threats addressed by the design.
+    pub const THREATS: &'static [&'static str] = &[
+        "T1: Privilege escalation via repair adding new capabilities",
+        "T2: Code injection via model-generated repair proposals",
+        "T3: Supply-chain compromise via path traversal beyond extension root",
+        "T4: Silent behavioral drift from opaque automated repairs",
+        "T5: Loss of auditability preventing incident forensics",
+        "T6: Governance decay from undocumented safety invariants",
+    ];
+
+    /// Why fail-closed was chosen.
+    pub const FAIL_CLOSED_RATIONALE: &'static str = "\
+Any uncertainty in repair safety defaults to denial. A broken extension \
+that remains broken is safer than a repaired extension that silently \
+escalates privileges or introduces semantic drift. The cost of a false \
+negative (missed repair) is low; the cost of a false positive (unsafe \
+repair applied) is catastrophic.";
+
+    /// Key safety invariants enforced by the system.
+    pub const INVARIANTS: &'static [&'static str] = &[
+        "I1: Repairs never add capabilities absent from the original extension",
+        "I2: All file paths stay within the extension root (monotonicity)",
+        "I3: Model proposals are restricted to whitelisted PatchOp primitives",
+        "I4: Structural validity is verified via SWC parse before activation",
+        "I5: Every repair decision is recorded in the append-only audit ledger",
+        "I6: Canary rollback triggers automatically on SLO violation",
+    ];
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Operator rollout and incident playbook (bd-k5q5.9.8.2)
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Operational procedure for repair mode selection.
+pub struct OperatorPlaybook;
+
+impl OperatorPlaybook {
+    /// Available repair modes and when to use each.
+    pub const MODE_GUIDANCE: &'static [(&'static str, &'static str)] = &[
+        (
+            "Off",
+            "Disable all automated repairs. Use when investigating a repair-related incident.",
+        ),
+        (
+            "Suggest",
+            "Log repair suggestions without applying. Use during initial rollout or audit.",
+        ),
+        (
+            "AutoSafe",
+            "Apply only safe (path-remap) repairs automatically. Default for production.",
+        ),
+        (
+            "AutoStrict",
+            "Apply both safe and aggressive repairs. Use only with explicit approval.",
+        ),
+    ];
+
+    /// Canary rollout procedure.
+    pub const CANARY_PROCEDURE: &'static [&'static str] = &[
+        "1. Create overlay artifact from repair pipeline",
+        "2. Verify all proofs pass (structural, capability, semantic, conformance)",
+        "3. Transition to Canary state with initial overlay_percent (e.g., 10%)",
+        "4. Monitor health signals for canary_window_ms (default: 300_000)",
+        "5. If SLO violated → automatic rollback",
+        "6. If canary window passes → promote to Stable",
+        "7. Record all transitions in audit ledger",
+    ];
+
+    /// Incident response steps.
+    pub const INCIDENT_RESPONSE: &'static [&'static str] = &[
+        "1. Set repair_mode to Off immediately",
+        "2. Export forensic bundle for the affected extension",
+        "3. Review audit ledger for the repair timeline",
+        "4. Check verification bundle for proof failures",
+        "5. Inspect health signals that triggered rollback",
+        "6. Root-cause the repair rule or model proposal",
+        "7. File ADR amendment if safety invariant was violated",
+    ];
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Developer guide for adding safe repair rules (bd-k5q5.9.8.3)
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Developer guide for contributing new repair rules.
+pub struct DeveloperGuide;
+
+impl DeveloperGuide {
+    /// Steps to add a new deterministic repair rule.
+    pub const ADD_RULE_CHECKLIST: &'static [&'static str] = &[
+        "1. Define a RepairPattern variant with clear trigger semantics",
+        "2. Define a RepairRule with: id, name, pattern, description, risk, ops",
+        "3. Risk must be Safe unless the rule modifies code (then Aggressive)",
+        "4. Add the rule to REPAIR_RULES static registry",
+        "5. Implement matching logic in the extension loader",
+        "6. Add unit tests covering: match, no-match, edge cases",
+        "7. Add integration test with real extension fixture",
+        "8. Verify monotonicity: rule must not escape extension root",
+        "9. Verify capability monotonicity: rule must not add capabilities",
+        "10. Run full conformance suite to check for regressions",
+    ];
+
+    /// Anti-patterns to avoid.
+    pub const ANTI_PATTERNS: &'static [(&'static str, &'static str)] = &[
+        (
+            "Unconstrained path rewriting",
+            "Always validate target paths are within extension root via verify_repair_monotonicity()",
+        ),
+        (
+            "Model-generated code execution",
+            "Model proposals must use PatchOp primitives only — never eval or Function()",
+        ),
+        (
+            "Skipping verification",
+            "Every repair must pass the full VerificationBundle gate before activation",
+        ),
+        (
+            "Mutable audit entries",
+            "AuditLedger is append-only — never expose delete or update methods",
+        ),
+        (
+            "Implicit capability grants",
+            "compute_capability_proof() must show no Added deltas for the repair to pass",
+        ),
+    ];
+
+    /// Testing expectations for new rules.
+    pub const TESTING_EXPECTATIONS: &'static [&'static str] = &[
+        "Unit test: rule matches intended pattern and rejects non-matching input",
+        "Unit test: generated PatchOps have correct risk classification",
+        "Integration test: repair applied to real extension fixture succeeds",
+        "Monotonicity test: repaired path stays within extension root",
+        "Capability test: compute_capability_proof returns Monotonic",
+        "Semantic test: compute_semantic_parity returns Equivalent or AcceptableDrift",
+        "Conformance test: extension still passes conformance replay after repair",
+    ];
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Governance checklist for CI/release (bd-k5q5.9.8.4)
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// A single governance check item.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
+pub struct GovernanceCheck {
+    /// Check identifier.
+    pub id: String,
+    /// Human-readable description.
+    pub description: String,
+    /// Whether the check passed.
+    pub passed: bool,
+    /// Detail message (empty if passed).
+    pub detail: String,
+}
+
+/// Result of running the governance checklist.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
+pub struct GovernanceReport {
+    /// Individual check results.
+    pub checks: Vec<GovernanceCheck>,
+    /// Number of checks that passed.
+    pub passed_count: usize,
+    /// Total number of checks.
+    pub total_count: usize,
+}
+
+impl GovernanceReport {
+    /// True if all governance checks passed.
+    pub const fn all_passed(&self) -> bool {
+        self.passed_count == self.total_count
+    }
+
+    /// Return failing checks.
+    pub fn failures(&self) -> Vec<&GovernanceCheck> {
+        self.checks.iter().filter(|c| !c.passed).collect()
+    }
+}
+
+/// Run the governance checklist against current system state.
+///
+/// Checks:
+/// 1. Repair registry has at least one rule.
+/// 2. All rule IDs are non-empty.
+/// 3. ADR invariants are non-empty (documentation exists).
+/// 4. Audit ledger is available (can be constructed).
+/// 5. Telemetry collector is available (can be constructed).
+/// 6. `VerificationBundle` checks all four proof layers.
+pub fn run_governance_checklist() -> GovernanceReport {
+    let mut checks = Vec::new();
+
+    // Check 1: Registry has rules.
+    checks.push(GovernanceCheck {
+        id: "GOV-001".to_string(),
+        description: "Repair registry contains at least one rule".to_string(),
+        passed: !REPAIR_RULES.is_empty(),
+        detail: if REPAIR_RULES.is_empty() {
+            "REPAIR_RULES is empty".to_string()
+        } else {
+            String::new()
+        },
+    });
+
+    // Check 2: All rule IDs are non-empty.
+    let empty_ids: Vec<_> = REPAIR_RULES
+        .iter()
+        .filter(|r| r.id.is_empty())
+        .map(|r| r.description)
+        .collect();
+    checks.push(GovernanceCheck {
+        id: "GOV-002".to_string(),
+        description: "All repair rules have non-empty IDs".to_string(),
+        passed: empty_ids.is_empty(),
+        detail: if empty_ids.is_empty() {
+            String::new()
+        } else {
+            format!("Rules with empty IDs: {empty_ids:?}")
+        },
+    });
+
+    // Check 3: ADR exists.
+    checks.push(GovernanceCheck {
+        id: "GOV-003".to_string(),
+        description: "Architecture ADR is defined".to_string(),
+        passed: !LisrAdr::INVARIANTS.is_empty(),
+        detail: String::new(),
+    });
+
+    // Check 4: ADR threats are documented.
+    checks.push(GovernanceCheck {
+        id: "GOV-004".to_string(),
+        description: "Threat model is documented".to_string(),
+        passed: !LisrAdr::THREATS.is_empty(),
